title: Why Perfectionism Is Sabotaging Your Work-Life Balance (And What to Do Instead)
guestBio: Cherylanne is the founder of Brilliant Balance and host of the Brilliant Balance Podcast. A former 15-year P&G marketing executive with a Cornell B.S. and Emory MBA, she's a trusted advisor to female leaders and a board member of the National Association of Women in AI.
excerpt: Work-life balance isn't about perfection—it's about core strength. Cherylanne Skolnicki reveals why high-achieving women feel like they're breaking in the middle and shares the identity shift that actually fixes it.
metaDescription: Discover why perfectionism kills work-life balance for ambitious women and learn the core strength approach that prevents burnout.
keyTakeaways: Balance is dynamic core strength, not equal sides of a pie chart.
The skills that got you to the top will burn you out if you don't evolve them.
Perfectionism is an identity marker that must be consciously laid down at your peak.
High care plus low trust equals an inability to delegate effectively.
The mental load is a systems problem, not a discipline problem.
AI can translate invisible labor into shared work at home.
Distance yourself from outcomes and stay focused on the work itself.

pullQuotes.text: Balance is core strength, not equal sides. It's a dynamic, ballerina-like stability that lets you carry a big, full life and still enjoy it.
Control is a symptom of caring; trust is the bridge that releases it.
The skills that got you here will burn you out at the peak.

timestamps.label: The question every ambitious woman wrestles with
Cherylanne's pivot from P&G to Brilliant Balance
The complexity curve and the peak years
The patterns that lead high-achievers to burnout
The double-click method: stop, diagnose, re-engineer
Why women can't sit out the AI revolution
Perfectionism as an identity marker that must be laid down
High care + low trust = an inability to delegate
The free front door podcast strategy
Don't read the comments — distance from the outcome

quiz.title: What's Your Balance Superpower?
quiz.description: Discover your natural approach to managing life's complexity and learn how to leverage your strengths while building sustainable systems.

A.name: The Orchestrator
A.description: You have an incredible ability to see the big picture and coordinate moving pieces. Your superpower is creating harmony across multiple domains of life. However, you might be carrying too much of the mental load and struggling to trust others with your systems. This episode will help you learn how to translate your invisible work into shared systems and build the trust needed to delegate effectively.

B.name: The Perfectionist
B.description: Your attention to detail and high standards have been key to your success, but they may be working against you in this season of life. You excel at delivering exceptional results but struggle when good enough would actually be better. This episode will help you understand how to lay down perfectionism as an identity marker and embrace core strength over rigid control.

C.name: The Adapter
C.description: You're naturally flexible and great at pivoting when circumstances change. Your superpower is resilience and the ability to find creative solutions. However, you might be adapting so much that you're losing sight of your own needs and boundaries. This episode will help you learn how to maintain your center while still being responsive to life's demands.

D.name: The Systematizer
D.description: You instinctively create processes and structures to manage complexity. Your superpower is building efficient systems that scale. However, you might be over-systematizing or creating systems that only you can manage. This episode will help you learn how to create truly sustainable systems and develop the core strength to handle what can't be systematized.

questions.question: When your day gets derailed by unexpected demands, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Mentally reorganize everyone else's priorities to accommodate the change
Feel frustrated that things aren't going according to your plan
Quickly adjust and figure out what can be moved or dropped
Look for a process or system that could prevent this in the future

questions.question: The thing that exhausts you most about managing your current responsibilities is:

options.text: Keeping track of everything in your head and feeling like you can't turn off
Redoing work that others did because it wasn't up to your standards
Constantly shifting priorities and never feeling like you can get ahead
Spending more time creating systems than actually getting things done

questions.question: When someone offers to help with something important to you, you typically:

options.text: Say yes but then worry about whether they'll remember all the details
Hesitate because you know you could do it better and faster yourself
Gratefully accept and adjust your expectations to match their approach
Accept but spend significant time creating instructions or training them

questions.question: Your biggest fear about not being in control of important outcomes is:

options.text: That important details will slip through the cracks and affect everyone
That the quality won't meet the standards that your reputation depends on
That you won't be able to course-correct quickly when things go wrong
That the lack of consistency will create chaos in your carefully built systems

questions.question: When you think about work-life balance, you most want:

options.text: To feel like you can be present in each area without constantly thinking about the others
To maintain high standards in every area without feeling like you're constantly falling short
To have the flexibility to focus where you're needed most without guilt
To have reliable systems that run smoothly so you can focus on what matters most

questions.question: The compliment you receive most often is that you:

options.text: Never seem to drop the ball and always remember what others forget
Deliver exceptional quality work that exceeds expectations
Handle unexpected challenges with grace and find creative solutions
Create order from chaos and help others be more efficient

transcript: Let's have some fun. >> Welcome back to the Manager podcast where we explore leadership, ambition, and the invisible work women do to build extraordinary lives. Today's guest is someone who has spent years helping high achieving women answer a question so many of us wrestle with. How do you build a big life without burning yourself out in the process? Joining me today is Cheryl Anne Skolniki, founder of Brilliant Balance, a leadership coach, entrepreneur, and host of the Brilliant Balance podcast. Cheryl and spent years in corporate leadership roles before pivoting to build a coaching company dedicated to helping ambitious women create lives that feel as good on the inside as they look on the outside. Her work focuses on helping women manage the intersection of career, leadership, family, and personal fulfillment without feeling like they are constantly dropping the ball somewhere. And as many of you know, here on the manager, we talk a lot about the invisible labor women carry, the mental load, the planning, the organizing, and how those skills can actually become leadership superpowers. Today, we're going to explore what balance really means for modern women, why so many high achieving women still feel overwhelmed, lessons she's learned from interviewing hundreds of successful women, and what we're going to pull back the curtain on the business of podcasting. >> Cheryl an, welcome to the manager podcast. >> I'm just delighted to be here. This will be f so fun. >> Well, I'd love to start with your story. what led you from corporate leadership into creating brilliant balance? >> So I it's such an important kind of part of my origin story I think is that I did spend 15 years working for Proctor and Gamble um right out of college I went to work for them as a total baby and I really thought I was going to stay in a corporate career forever. I was a very diligent corporate ladder climber and at some point I think when I looked at the next few rungs up the ladder I just didn't want to be there. I just was like that is not the work that I really feel called to do. I don't feel like that's the difference I want to see in the world. The dent I want to make in the universe so to speak. I was you know full disclosure working on like laundry detergent and something about you know how much does it really matter which brand you use. you have to care about that a whole lot to devote your career to that. So I started thinking about the problems I really wanted to solve and most of them were centered in challenges that I was seeing women face um that either I was facing myself or had faced previously or women around me were facing. And the first business that I felt called to start was in the health and wellness space because so many of my peers were struggling with figuring out how to feed themselves well, their families well, how to fit in, you know, fitness and movement. And I was really passionate about some of the transformation I'd seen in my own life and wanted to share it. And then one thing sort of led to another where I think the scope of issues that I was passionate about and equipped to solve for women continued to expand until it became what is today Brilliant Balance. >> That's amazing. It's always amazing to see how one thing leads to another with women. And so we kind of follow our heart and our passion and our purpose towards where we're supposed to be as we're evolving as human beings. It's pretty amazing. It has 100% been my experience, but everyone thinks you have to have a perfect 10-year strategic plan and I thought that when and it's just not true. It's just not true. >> Yeah, I love that. Yeah. My life has never been written in Sharpie. I always think I'm going to do one thing and then it's like, oh, or maybe I'm going to pivot and do this instead. >> Right. Right. >> But having like flexibility in your life will bring you happiness in the long run. Being flexible with the changes that are going to come. >> I agree completely. >> Yeah. When you look back at that transition, was there a moment when you realized you wanted to help other women design their lives differently? Yes. And I think that that moment was when I realized that it was not about the food at all. Like if you remember the first business was about really helping women transform their own relationship with food, often a problematic relationship. And then you there's so much shame around what we're feeding our families. And I think through my work to explore the why to really get underneath like why is this such an issue a persistent issue? Why is there so much shame around it? Right underneath the surface of that were all of the other things that they were carrying, right? Like it wasn't a discipline issue. It wasn't a a choice issue. It was like it was much more foundational. And that was I think probably I haven't really thought about it that way before, but that was probably the pivot to say I think we're going to have to go a little deeper here. >> I love it. I love it. I see that in my work too. I'm like, "Huh? Are we going right?" Yes. Is this a symptom of some sort of cultural conditioning that we have? >> Yes, it is. Um, the word balance can sometimes feel like a myth. What does brilliant balance actually mean in practice? Oh, this is my favorite question because I think sometimes I've tossed around the idea of just burning the whole brand to the ground because people hate the word balance so much. I think I really feel a sense of reclaiming that word because I think of balance as a dynamic practice almost staying in balance. Um like core strength, right? The way like a ballerina would have core strength versus you know equal slices of a pie chart so to speak or equally balanced sides of a scale. There's nothing static about balance in the life of a woman ever at any point in the life cycle. And I've thought a lot about sort of the cycle of a woman's life along what I think of as the complexity curve. And that complexity sort of increases steadily and peaks at a certain number of years where you're sort of carrying the maximum amount of career responsibilities, family responsibilities, household responsibilities, community responsibilities, right? We're just bearing a lot in these peak years before we start to re-edit our lives and sort of resettle on ourselves at some point. Those peak years are really where brilliant balance principles apply. And so I think of it as the collection of practices that help women stay like you said it at the beginning of the episode helps them maintain a big full life while also having the freedom to enjoy it. Right? It's a both and. There is a skill set or a collection of practices that helps to enable that because most women in those peak years are feeling so much tension between their ambition like just call it what it is and the demands of their family life. The tension between those two is like it's just breaking them in the middle. And I'm trying to reset the balance among those three things. Your professional ambition, the pull of your family, like the gravitational pull of your family, and the need to protect some space for yourself to be a human in the midst of all of that. >> Yeah. I mean, I feel it. It's I'm there right now, you know. Absolutely. You are living at the peak. >> I'm in the peak. Yeah. Where you're really trying, you really are. You're trying to figure out how can I still be me, be fully in myself, not burn out, and also feel like I don't suck at everything like, you know, how can I feel like I'm being a good mom? I'm being a good wife. I'm being a good businesswoman. I'm able to give enough of myself to all the things that I'm passionate about, which are all of those things in my life. You know, and it's sometimes it's it's not always hard and we can be really hard on ourselves about it and finding those skills that ambitious women. We're ambitious women. So, you work with a lot of us. What are the most common patterns you see when women come to you feeling overwhelmed? >> Yeah. So, I think the first thing that is probably the most broadly seen is that they're not a human. Like, they don't have human standards that apply to them, right? They've somehow transcended human needs of like sleep and good food and you know daylight. That sort of abdication of self of thinking like I'll just take care of me later is the first thing to go. And I think it's kind of it's a problem of naive really. I think it happens a little earlier stage in this. I think later in the peak women start to really get it. Oh, if I don't level set on some of this stuff, I'm not going to make it. It is the path to burnout. But truly those practices, I think of them as resilience rituals that allow women to bear a greater load of stress without breaking under the pressure of it are so foundational. It is widespread to ignore or underserve those things. So I think that's a really big one. I think tools to manage the mental load that come with this chapter of life so prevalent as to be cliched that there is a mental load. We cannot we're not going to say there's not. There is. It's significant and I think but there's a lot of self-lame and self-loathing around like why can't I and the reality is because it is a ridiculous expectation that you would keep all of this in your head. So tools around managing that I think the calendar chaos that comes with this stage is so prevalent. There is just there are so many people's calendars. I mean you have more than mine right? I have three children and a spouse and my own and a team of multiple people and I see all of their calendars all the time and I am, you know, I have two parents who are thankfully still alive and live close by. That's a lot of calendar chaos. And so managing to have a level of control over that and decision-m that's a skill that has to be built. And then I think emotional regulation. I think our ability to stay grounded and emotionally centered when there are a lot of people's roller coasters, right? taking us on the ride with them and learning that we can stand at the base of the roller coaster and not necessarily get on the ride is a really important skill in this season if we want to kind of keep our sanity. So, those are some of the biggest. >> I love that. Yeah. Not react and don't react. Don't overreact to anything. That's sort of my the only way I can have five kids and a husband is, you know, okay, observe their emotional states and not really react to it. It's kind of learning to hold that >> responsible for solving for them. Like I think we take so much responsibility for solving everyone else's problems of all kinds, including their emotional challenges and just learning that they're learning those skills, you know, and we can support and witness to your point, observe without having to bear the responsibility of solving for it. That's that's kind of revolutionary. >> Yeah, absolutely. And on my show, we talk a lot about invisible labor, the mental load we carry at home and at work. How do you see that showing up in your coaching practice? I mean, it's omnipresent, right? Again, I think it's foundational to some of the self-criticism that can quickly elevate to shame that women are carrying around is why can't I carry all of this in my head and get it all done? And I think we just don't have mechanisms for where to put it, how to get an assist where we need it, how to sort of establish a a remote brain that we don't have to carry everything around in our own. And and there are kind of hard skills there that we're just not taught. So coaching it shows up a lot of equip. I think I in my coaching practice it's less therapy style and more equipping. Like that's I'm really pragmatic and I want women to have solutions that they can like walk out and apply. So we spend a lot of time talking about how do you how do you lower that mental load and make it more manageable and what are the actual tools and practices that do that. Nice. So like applying systems around these things better systems, management systems. >> Yes. a thousand% >> around the invisible work because Yeah. Yeah. >> around all of it. Yeah. And I think, you know, I have this little phrase that I think has become I don't know where I picked it up. It's widespread now, but to just when something's not working to double click on it, right? Just to pause and say like, hold on instead of just continuing to go on this way and complaining about how hard it is and how it's not working. Like, let's just stop for a second and sort of diagnose what is really going on here and then re-engineer. Is there a better way to approach this ongoing? And it's wild how often the solution's right there. Like as soon as we take a few minutes to just get underneath it, there's a solution and we can kind of move forward with a better way. And I I feel like that happens every day inside of conversations with clients and in my own household. And we're seeing such a big transformation with technology right now, too. So there's a lot more every day. It seems like there's a new tool that we could rely on to automate some of this invisible labor. >> Sure. I mean, the the LLMs alone, right? I mean, just using Claude or using chat to say like, "Okay, I'm going to equip an agent to do these things for me." I mean, we just didn't have it a few years ago. Even programming, I was saying to someone on my team today, programming that I released a few years ago, it's completely outdated. I mean, it needs to completely be revised and refreshed to be relevant today because I would be underserving the tools that are available now that just weren't there. >> Yeah. I mean, I love that we have the calendars that we have like having a Google calendar where you can integrate with everybody in the family. That helps a lot. It really does. pulling things on and off. >> Yeah, on and off. And I have multiple calendars, too, so that I can put stuff on my work calendar so my husband doesn't have to see certain things, you know, and it's not so the calendars don't get congested. Making sure you're doing calendar management, basically. Yeah, that's right. >> Yeah, cuz that way you can still see what you're doing. But I think technology and women and the the digital revolution that we're in right now is going to have a huge impact on women in the way that we elevate and automate. >> I think so, too. And I think, you know, my caution to listeners is and just to women in general in my community is I think we have to be careful that we don't fall prey to like the tropes, right, of just throwing up our hands and being like, h, it's going so fast and I don't understand it. Like, you just can't. This is not one that you can sit out, right? I think a lot of women got trained during the social media evolution of like, oh, maybe I can sit this one out. Maybe I don't need to do the next platform. And I think that was true in social media for sure. There were entire eras that it was fine to sit out. This is not that one. Like I would be real careful about getting too far behind in what the AI tools can do for us because if you're not using them and not figuring out use cases that are relevant to your career and to your household, you will not keep up. That's just an imperative. And so I think I'm an evangelist about that at this point. I love that. I recently joined the board of the the National Association of Women in AI and NAWI's mission is to help equip women with the tools to use AI because because of the wage gap that already exists. >> That's right. >> If we don't adapt and adopt these te this technology, we the gap is going to get further and further between men and women. And we're already seeing CEO level women who are getting fired because they don't have AI skills. That's right. >> So this is big big mission for me is to make sure that we don't sleep on this one. We need to embrace it. We've it's not going anywhere. Even it's don't be afraid of the robots taking over the world. Be afraid of being left behind in the past. >> Yes. And I I think especially when you think about women who still more often than men have take time out of the workforce, right? Let's just use maternity leave as an example or any other kind of caregiving leave. The pace of change is so radical that I mean I took three year-long maternity leaves in my earlier adulthood. If I would have been out for the last year, I mean the world I remember I left and nobody had a Blackberry and I came back and everybody had a Blackberry and I was like what happened? I mean right that was a long time ago but now a year if I had been out and not keeping up with the curve I mean it's just the pace is wild. So, I really I think it's great that you're doing that work and I'm anxious to hear what comes out of it. >> Yeah, it's just I couldn't be more of a proponent for women embracing this technology is so important and as as we learn to have brilliant balance in our lives, technology is going to be a big part of that. I think >> it is. >> It is. It's an enabler, right? And maybe like levels the playing field to some extent. I think women have complained for a long time about, you know, my husband doesn't do this and I'm just the only one who sees it. And I keep saying like you have a digital brain, a digital assistant now that can bear some of that for you also that can play the intermediary between you and your partner on providing direction, translating from what's in your head to what you need them to be able to keep up with and do. Like there's really a lot of use cases. >> Absolutely. No, you're so right. You could use that calendar also to show him all of the things that you're doing that maybe he could didn't see before. You know, I think that that was one of the big things in our house was I don't think he really saw all the things I was doing because I was culturally programmed to do them so quietly. So, like a little fairy, you know, and I think that we so many of us are that way. So, but it's kind of nice if you're looking for your partner to be a partner. A lot of times they just need to see what's actually going on and, you know, take the magic away and show them the reality of the household life so that they can meet you where you need them to be. But one of the things that I've learned from hosting my podcast is that when you talk to enough high performing women, you start noticing patterns from all the interviews you've done on Brilliant Balance podcast. What are some of the most interesting mindset shifts or concepts you've learned from your guests? Oh, that's a great question. One of the things that I think I see over and over again is perfectionism as a pattern. I think a lot of us who were high achievers, sort of deemed extraordinary early on, which is often the audience that we're kind of rolling with, those women really developed that exceptionality through perfectionism. And it's worded early in life, right? You have nothing to manage but yourself. And so you can do it with a level of exactitude that just doesn't translate as you're kind of climbing that complexity curve. And so I think that the women who successfully shift to the peak who like really are bearing that stage of life well have learned imperfect action, right? How do I get out of the starting gates without having everything being perfectly set? And also sort of like definition of enough. What is it? What is good enough in certain areas of my life so that I can put the full force of my talent against the most essential things and I can let other things be good enough for now. And by the way, that can vacasillate over time. The shift out of perfectionism into sort of this variable level of like even excellence that's required I think is something that the women who seem to be carrying the biggest lives and having the most success have been have been really really skilled at doing. And it's not natural because you're letting go of something that's become like an identity marker in order to do that. But it's really powerful. >> I can see that. I can absolutely see that. Our family motto is when good enough will do. And I think that having a mom who kind of has always been able to jump into imperfect action modeled that behavior. So I think that's part of my resilience. Part of my success has come from my ability to not I'm really not a perfectionist. I think I come off as one. But I think I'm actually okay with being okay. Just okay. If I can do it okay enough. >> I don't know you well but you seem like somebody who's into breath. Like I want to get a lot of things done. And I'm sort of multi-passionate. And again, what maybe like inherently comes with that is I can't be that deep and kind of like tweaking any one thing overly to have that big of a life, that much breath. But when people try, I think this is the really important insight where women get to burnout or get crushed is trying to maintain that standard that kind of got them there. like the thing that was effective in the climb as they're kind of like building, you know, acquiring promotions and adding that you have to lay that skill down and develop a new one to really crest the curve. And I I just don't think we do it organically. I think you're so right. 100%. I see it all the time. I see it with the women on my show who've burnt out, the ones who've like had to make huge pivots in their lives. A lot of times it comes from perfectionism and like the inability to delegate or that overont control, you know, an inability to let go of control of things. Yeah. >> Yeah. It's like you get to a point in your life where there's so too many things to control that you really can't do it all by yourself. >> Yeah. And I think the control I don't know how you see this, but the women that I'm most often with, but control is like a symptom of caring. They care a lot about everything. >> So, and they don't always trust that other people will care as much. And so when you have high care, low trust, you can't release it. So really sometimes it's more about learning to build trust or surrounding yourself with people that are really accept that are excellent that you do trust that allows you to release the control. I'm not going to release control at all to somebody I don't think is competent. So putting yourself around people where there's a lot of competence can really be the bridge that helps you learn that skill. Oh, that's such a good point, Cherylyn. That's been my biggest thing in life is I surround myself with highly competent people because I delegate a lot. because it's the only way I could do everything I do. So, yeah. I mean, who are It's a good question. Who are some of the most influential women you've had on your show that really left a lasting impression on you? So, I love each and every person who's been on the show, we've had guest coaches inside of the Brilliant Balance community. The first person who always comes to mind when I think about that, like my guests, is Katherine Morgan Schoffler. She wrote a book called The Perfectionist Guide to Losing Control. You know, Katherine's not as wellknown as some of the other people that I've had on the show, but the work that she did through that book is so exceptional, and the conversation that I had with her was so powerful and and really like mind-opening to not just to me, but to all the women in our community that she's really a standout. I had a great conversation with Martha Beck when she released her work on integrity and like the path to integrity. That was a really exceptional conversation. And I had a great conversation with Jill Bolty Taylor on her work after her stroke like her the work is called my stroke of insight and just a brilliant mind that had an insight after a stroke. I mean she do you know her work? I do not. So it's unbelievable. She is a physician who I think she's a neuroscientist who had a stroke and was able to ascertain what was happening to her in real time because she had had the training but also lost her complete left brain functionality. So she came out of her stroke entirely rightrain like no language, no processing, no reason like and it took years of recovery work to get it back and is now back like speaking and writing and teach all the thing. Absolutely fascinating story. Um she was one of the great I mean I could go on for I've been doing the podcast for seven or eight years. I have lots and lots of really great interviews but those are a few standouts. >> That's amazing. That's so it's the one of the best parts about being a podcaster I think is meeting these getting these incredible stories and having this knowledge transfer. I just thought of another one I have to say is Don Salem. Her story of what she has lived through and survived is so ridiculous and remarkable and the work that she's doing in healthcare is phenomenal. I think she was at the Mayo Clinic but I think she's just changed to another location. She's another one of those people like I will probably only meet a handful of people in my life ever who are like her who have this kind of frequency that they're vibrating at. Like she is remarkable like really a remarkable human. >> What is her work um center around? >> So it's really centered around health and wellness and taking exceptional care of yourself so that you don't end up in some of these health crises like cancer prevention is a big piece of it. She's just remarkable. Yeah. >> Amazing. Amazing. Let's talk a little bit about the business side of podcasting because a lot of women listening are curious about this world. So, you've been podcasting what, six or seven years? Seven or eight years. Seven. Yeah. Seven or eight years. And it looks glamorous from the outside, but we know that there's a lot of work behind the scenes. So, have you ever had a moment where you thought about giving up? >> No. Not in the podcast. >> Yeah. I The podcast is one of my favorite parts of the things I do. I love it so much. I mean, I would say there were phases over those years where I was dealing with a level of personal crisis in like my family that challenged the discipline that I have around the podcast. You know, I've produced an episode every week, just one episode a week, but every week for those years and I write all of them myself. I have a lot of solo episodes. They are not all interviews. And I think solo episodes carry their own weight because you're really you have a creative burden in a solo episode that you don't have in an interview. And they're they're hard for different reasons. So that was difficult and I had dig myself out of a ditch a few times with like you know what it is okay to air an encore episode that you've aired before to get yourself through a difficult week like a little bit of self-compassion but I genuinely love the format of podcasting and I have a great team around me. I think people don't always can't picture like what happens in a podcast. You and I do them a little bit differently but I deliver a raw audio file to a production team that takes it from there. So all of the bells and whistles and cutting excerpts out and getting show notes done and you know distributing it to all the platforms like I don't do that work. So I get to do the part of it that I love which is create the content, record the episode or have the interview and then sort of step aside and let the let my team go from there. And that is a very important piece of the puzzle. >> Yeah, definitely. I always say you got to stay in your realm of genius. Why reinvent the wheel if someone else is better at doing it? That's just a business philosophy of mine. like I don't edit. I'm not good at that. I don't enjoy doing that. I hire that out. And then I have a social media guy that we have created an AI integration for social media and you know, of course, automated course. Right. Right. Right. Right. But I think that it's, you know, again, what was attractive to me about podcasting is I was blogging for years before podcasting. And, you know, I'm a decent writer. I like writing. I'm probably going to be doing more writing soon. I really did like it, but it is a it's a labor. Writing a piece that you're really going to polish is laborious. I am verbally gifted, right? It is not hard for me to string sentences together that like come out of my mouth the way I want them to. So podcasting was a really natural forum for me because I'm not stressed about having to talk to someone or talk to a microphone. In fact, it's easier, right? Because I'm not overthinking it the way I might do in writing. And so for me, the shift from blogging to podcasting actually felt like help. Now, I did not want to do video and I was really resistant, really, really resistant to doing anything on video. And that took a while. My podcast producer teases me all the time because he's like, "Holy God, you did not want to get a camera." I was like, "I know." I love that. >> Hand it forward. >> Solid solid advice. I love love love that because this is you know this is a platform that does just go on and on and on like you don't just record it and it goes out that one week and that's it. It's like you go you put it out that week and it's out there forever and people are discovering I don't know when you look at your statistics I mean when we look at our listening stats what people listen to in any given week is not the show I sent that week it's all the stuff that search led them to. So you get this array of listenership across your archives that's really important to be aware of. It's not just what am I saying this week? Yeah, there's a big surge in current content, but it's there's so many people who are working their way through archives or finding their way through search to a particular episode and that's their entry point. Well, I just thought, you know, we are entrepreneurs. We're women and so I'm sure I actually meet a lot of women who are like, "Oh, I want to have a podcast. What does that entail? Would you help me produce it?" all the kinds of questions like that and as we as podcasters have some insight but I feel like I know a little bit you know a little bit more. And so I think if you and I can sort of it's really not that hard, right? The ability to get into these there's the barriers to entry are so low to start a podcast or start a Tik Tok channel or whatever you want to do. I think like people we create manufactur so much fear about how hard it must be. And I sound like Lwood. It's like what? Like it's hard. It's not. It's just not, you know. So I think if we can debunk that a bit, we'll probably get more people out of the starting blocks. >> Heck yeah. Yeah. It's the scariest part about starting a podcast was the starting a podcast part of being, you know, being okay with being a voice and sharing my voice. And even the book, like writing the book was easy, but the day that we published, the day my book went live on Amazon, I literally thought I was going to die. Like it was this most ter and I, you know me, I've done big things. It was terrifying. And it was kind of the same. I mean, kind of the same with a podcast, but I kind of produce and then I don't observe it too much. I just let it go and do its thing and I try not to Oh, I never listen to them. Yeah. Yeah. I really try to distance myself from the outcome and just be on the journey of it and try to stay in that mindset of this is the adventure. I'm going to enjoy the moment that I get to be with my guest and share these amazing this insight and go and find these answers to these questions together and have these amazing convers highle conversations and then it's just out in the universe I release it the outcome of it. I agree and you hear people talk about that like I think that probably women listening have heard in various kind of entertainment angles of the entertainment industry people talk about not reading the comments or not looking at not watching their movies if they produce but I really think it's important that you know you've produced a quality piece of work and there's probably a cadence of listening to your own work to see where can you get better. I realized I said the word right a lot and I'm I would really like to get that out of my cadence of speaking and and it's because I heard someone else be interviewed that was really credible but he said right about every fifth word and I was and I thought to myself I'm going to go back and listen to a couple of things and see if I have anything and I thought oh no there it is. So, you have to hear yourself to hear, do you have anything that's coming out regularly that you kind of you want to get rid of, but not every episode and not reading every comment and not wondering why this one didn't land because there's always some crazy stupid thing that happened that has nothing to do with you that can get in the way of the statistics. So, you can really just whip yourself into a frenzy unnecessarily. But, I think we should always be trying to get better at our craft. For sure. >> Yeah, absolutely. And I think it was funny, but my first couple of episodes, my father is a big fan of my show. >> And he said, I forget what he he said, you got to give them don't say uh-huh, don't say. So I go back now and I'm okay. So now when I I'm very a lot more cautious about how I respond and you know, holding space and making sure that there's space for edits, too. >> That's right. >> You know, taking time with the words. Podcasters have to learn not to verbally respond to each other. This is something I hear when I'm being interviewed is I'm speaking and they want to give me verbal reinforcement because that's what we do in conversation. But you learn as a podcaster to give nonverbal head nod reinforcement, you know, okay sign silently because you know it's going to get picked up and then they have to edit you out the other person's audio be clean. content:
If you're an ambitious woman who feels like you're breaking in the middle, this conversation is for you. Aimee sits down with Cherylanne Skolnicki, founder of Brilliant Balance, to unpack why so many high-achieving women hit a wall—and what it actually takes to build sustainable success.

The problem isn't that you're not trying hard enough. The problem is that you're still using the same perfectionist playbook that got you to where you are, even though it's now actively working against you.

The Complexity Curve: Why Your Peak Years Feel Impossible

Cherylanne introduces a concept that will make everything click: the complexity curve. This is the period in your life where career demands, family responsibilities, household management, and community obligations all peak at the same time. You're not imagining it—this phase is genuinely more complex than anything you've navigated before.

The skills that served you in your twenties and early thirties—grinding harder, controlling every detail, pushing through exhaustion—become the very things that burn you out during your peak complexity years. You need a fundamentally different approach, and it starts with redefining what balance actually means.

Balance Is Core Strength, Not Equal Sides

Forget the pie chart version of work-life balance. Real balance isn't about equal slices of time devoted to each area of your life. Instead, think of it as core strength—the dynamic stability that allows a ballerina to hold challenging positions while making it look effortless.

This kind of balance is active, not passive. It requires you to constantly adjust, recalibrate, and make conscious choices about where to focus your energy. Some seasons will be career-heavy, others family-focused. The goal isn't perfect equilibrium; it's maintaining your center while carrying the full weight of an ambitious life.

The Identity Shift That Changes Everything

Here's the hard truth: perfectionism got you to where you are, but it will destroy you if you don't learn to lay it down. Perfectionism isn't just a habit—it's an identity marker. It's how you've proven your worth, demonstrated your competence, and earned your place at the table.

But at your peak complexity years, perfectionism becomes a liability. It keeps you micromanaging instead of delegating. It makes you redo work that's already good enough. It traps you in a cycle of high care plus low trust, which equals an inability to let anyone else carry the load.

The identity shift requires you to move from being the person who does everything perfectly to being the person who creates systems that work. It means valuing progress over perfection and recognizing that your worth isn't tied to your ability to control every outcome.

The Mental Load Is a Systems Problem

One of the most powerful insights from this conversation is Cherylanne's reframe of the mental load. The invisible labor that women carry—remembering appointments, managing household needs, tracking everyone's schedules—isn't a discipline problem. It's a systems problem.

Instead of trying to be better at carrying an impossible cognitive load, you need to create what Cherylanne calls a "remote brain." This could be digital tools, AI assistance, or simply documented processes that other people can follow. The goal is to translate invisible work into visible, shareable systems.

Why Women Can't Afford to Sit Out the AI Revolution

Cherylanne makes a compelling case for why ambitious women must engage with AI technology. The wage gap isn't just about unequal pay—it's about women being left behind as work itself transforms. AI has the potential to level the playing field by handling the administrative and organizational tasks that disproportionately fall on women's plates.

Think of AI as a partner that can translate your invisible labor into shared work. It can track family schedules, manage household inventories, and handle the thousand small decisions that currently live in your brain. This isn't about replacing human connection—it's about freeing up your mental bandwidth for the work that actually matters.

The Double-Click Method for Sustainable Change

When you notice yourself feeling overwhelmed or stretched thin, Cherylanne suggests the double-click method: stop, diagnose, and re-engineer. Instead of pushing through or accepting that "this is just how it is," pause and examine what's actually happening.

Are you trying to maintain control because you care deeply, or because you don't trust others to handle things well? Are you redoing work that was already acceptable because it wasn't perfect? Are you carrying mental load that could be systematized?

Most importantly, are you still using strategies that worked in a simpler season of life, even though they're now creating more problems than they solve?

Permission to Distance Yourself from Outcomes

Perhaps the most liberating insight from this conversation is Cherylanne's advice to distance yourself from outcomes and stay focused on the work itself. This doesn't mean you stop caring about results—it means you stop tying your identity and worth to things beyond your control.

You can control your effort, your systems, and your choices. You can't control other people's responses, market conditions, or unexpected life events. When you tie your sense of success to outcomes you can't control, you set yourself up for chronic anxiety and disappointment.

This episode is essential listening for any ambitious woman who's tired of feeling like she's constantly on the verge of breaking. The answer isn't to work harder or be more perfect—it's to build the core strength that allows you to carry a big, full life with grace.
